message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>COVID-19 vaccinations in children remains controversial. In the UK, as of August 2021, they have not been approved, except in a few limited circumstances. To date, little qualitative research exists to explain the reasons and nuances behind public attitudes on this issue.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Qualitative group and one-to-one online interviews were conducted with a diverse sample of 24 adults to explore their views on the issue of COVID-19 vaccination in children.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    COVID-19 vaccination in children was framed as a complex problem (a “minefield”). Six themes emerged to explain participants views: (1) Uncertainty over whether children can catch, transmit or be severely harmed by COVID-19; (2) Lower risk tolerance for unknown longer-term effects of the vaccine in children; (3) Association of the vaccine program with government’s handling of the pandemic
                    <jats:bold>;</jats:bold>
                    (4) Local social norms as a driver of hesitancy; (5) Vaccinating children as a way to protect vulnerable adults; (6) Children’s vaccination as parental choice.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Public attitudes to COVID-19 vaccination in children are likely to be met with more hesitancy compared to adult vaccinations. Public health communications will need to combat this hesitancy if vaccine uptake for children is to be pursued as a public health policy.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Public attitudes towards COVID-19 vaccination in children: A qualitative study

reference.unstructured: World Health Organization (2021) https://covid19.who.int/ https://covid19.who.int/ (accessed 27 July 2021)
UK Government (2021) GOV.UK Coronavirus (COVID-19) in the UK, vaccinations in the UK. https://coronavirus.data.gov.uk/details/vaccinations (accessed 27 July 2021)

reference.journal-title: Considering Mandatory Vaccination of Children for COVID-19 Pediatrics

reference.article-title: Herd immunity and vaccination of children for COVID-19
reference.journal-title: International journal of infectious diseases : IJID : official publication of the International Society for Infectious Diseases

reference.article-title: Using the framework method for the analysis of qualitative data in multi-disciplinary health research
